concepts.title: Monte Carlo for pricing — the idea

beats.text: An option's price today is the discounted expectation of its future payoff under the risk-neutral measure.

visual.sub: A price is an expectation you can estimate by averaging

beats.text: When the payoff depends on the whole path or on many assets, the integral becomes high-dimensional and no formula survives.
The estimator discounts the average of many simulated payoffs, and by the law of large numbers it converges to the true price.

visual.note: Unbiased: its expectation equals V0 for any N

beats.text: The standard error falls only as one over the square root of N, so halving the error costs four times the paths.
Adding one decimal digit of accuracy requires a hundred times more paths, which is why every later section reduces variance instead.

visual.label: more paths for one extra digit

beats.text: Grids and trees suffer the curse of dimensionality, but Monte Carlo error is independent of dimension.

concepts.title: Simulating payoffs under the risk-neutral measure

beats.text: Scenarios must be drawn under the risk-neutral measure, where the underlying drifts at the riskless rate rather than its real-world rate.

visual.sub: Generate futures under the measure Q

beats.text: For a European payoff one terminal price needs exactly one Gaussian draw, because the geometric Brownian motion solution is known exactly.

visual.note: Z ~ N(0,1); the Ito term makes E[S_T] = S0 e^{rT}

beats.text: When you need the whole path, you discretize the SDE over a time grid and step the log-price one Gaussian at a time.

visual.note: Log-Euler stays exact for GBM at any step size

beats.text: Stepping in log price keeps the scheme exact and the price strictly positive, so the only error is statistical.
A run of two hundred thousand paths prices a call within a tight confidence interval that matches the closed form.
On log-log axes the standard error is a straight line of slope minus one half, and raising the payoff spread lifts the whole line.
Since the error is the payoff spread over root N, you either run more paths or shrink the spread itself.

visual.sub: Shrink sigma_Y instead of growing N

beats.text: Antithetic variates pair every path driven by Z with a mirror path driven by minus Z and average the two payoffs.
For any payoff monotone in the shock the correlation is provably negative, so antithetics always help a vanilla call.
Control variates subtract a correlated quantity whose mean you already know, scaling the variance by one minus rho squared.

visual.note: The optimal coefficient is a regression slope

beats.text: A control correlated 0.99 with the payoff cuts the variance about fiftyfold, the single most powerful trick in the chapter.
Production pricers stack antithetics, a strong control, and quasi-random Sobol sequences that sweep the cube evenly.
Monte Carlo earns its keep where closed forms stop, on payoffs that depend on the whole trajectory the price took.
An Asian option pays on the average price over the monitoring dates, which damps both volatility and last-minute manipulation.

visual.note: A sum of lognormals has no closed form

beats.text: The geometric-average Asian does price in closed form and sits about 0.99 correlated with the arithmetic one, the textbook control.
A down-and-out call pays the usual amount unless the running minimum ever touches the barrier B, in which case it is worthless.

visual.note: The indicator zeroes the payoff on a breach

beats.text: Checking the barrier only at grid points misses intra-step crossings, so a coarse path systematically over-prices a knock-out.
Lookbacks, cliquets, autocallables, and worst-of baskets all use the same loop, only the payoff code changes.
A price is only half the job, because desks hedge with the Greeks and those derivatives must come out of the same simulation.
Bump-and-revalue reprices at two shifted spots and takes the slope, the universal but treacherous central difference.

visual.note: A central difference of two MC prices

beats.text: Too large a bump biases the estimate and too small a bump lets Monte Carlo noise blow up the variance like one over h squared.
The pathwise estimator differentiates the payoff for an unbiased delta in one pass whenever the payoff is Lipschitz.

visual.note: Fails at the kink of a digital

beats.text: Adjoint algorithmic differentiation computes all Greeks in one reverse pass, the same trick that powers backpropagation.
The common-seed rule fixes the most frequent bug: reuse the same random numbers across every revaluation so noise cancels.
